coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent probe based on FRET effect between carbon nanodots and gold nanoparticles for sensitive detection of thiourea
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Anqi
Chen, Guoqing
Yang, Taiqun
Ma, Chaoqun
Li, Lei
Gao, Hui
Gu, Jiao
Zhu, Chun
Wu, Yamin
Li, Xiaolin
Wei, Yitao
Huang, Anlan
Qiu, Xiaoqian
Xu, Jinzeng
Shen, Jialu
Zhong, Lvyuan
coredata.dc:description: Illegal abuse results in the presence of thiourea (TU) in soil, wastewater, and even fruits, which is harmful for the environment and human health. It has urgent practical significance to design an efficient and reliable probe for TU detection. Herein, a sensitive fluorescent probe with off–on response for harmful TU was reported. The probe was designed with fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect. Firstly, the CNDs were pre-combined with AuNPs and the fluorescence of CNDs was quenched due to the FRET effect. Upon addition of TU, the fluorescence of CNDs recovered due to the unbinding of CNDs and AuNPs, since the coordination interaction between TU and AuNPs is stronger than the electrostatic interaction among CNDs and AuNPs. Under the optimum parameters, a linear relationship was found between the relative fluorescence intensity of the probe and the concentration of TU in the range of 5.00 × 10-8-1.00 × 10-6 M (R2 = 0.9958), with the limit of detection (LOD) calculated to be 3.62 × 10-8 M. This proposed method is easy to operate and has excellent selectivity and sensitivity for TU, which can be effectively applied in environmental water and compound fruit-vegetable juice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Gold nanoparticles
Thiourea
Detection
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TU in real samples
Morphology and structural characterization of CNDs and AuNPs
Selectivity and anti-interference ability of CNDs + AuNPs
Detection of TU in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CNDs and AuNPs. (A) HRTEM image of CNDs. (B) Diameter distribution of CNDs. (C) HRTEM image of AuNPs. (D) Diameter distribution of AuNPs. (E) FT-IR spectrum of CNDs. (F) The survey XPS spectrum of CNDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs at different excitation wavelengths (365–420 nm). (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CNDs, CNDs + TU, CNDs + AuNPs and CNDs + AuNPs + TU. (C) Zeta potentials of CNDs, AuNPs and CNDs + AuNPs. (D) Fluorescence decay plots of CNDs and CNDs + AuNPs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs and fluorescence emission spectrum of CNDs (λex = 405 nm). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs, CNDs + TU, CNDs + AuNPs and CNDs + AuNPs + TU (λex = 405 nm). (C) HRTEM image of CNDs + AuNPs. (D) HRTEM image of CNDs + AuNPs + TU.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of CNDs + AuNPs in the presence of different concentrations of TU. (B) The relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity of CNDs + AuNPs + TU and the concentration of TU within 1.00 × 10-5 M. (Inset: the linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity of CNDs + AuNPs + TU and the concentration of TU in the range of 5.00 × 10-8-1.00 × 10-6 M).

(A) The fluorescence enhancement efficiency of CNDs + AuNPs in the presence of TU or one of these interferents. (B) The anti-interference ability of CNDs + AuNPs, where FBlank is the relative fluorescence intensity of CNDs + AuNPs with only TU and FBoth refers to the relative fluorescence intensity of CNDs + AuNPs with both TU and one of these interferents. (The concentration of TU and nitrogen-rich analogues is 2.00 × 10-6 M, and the concentration of the other interferents is 1.00 × 10-5 M).

Schematic illustration of CNDs + AuNPs fluorescent probe for TU detection. The photographs show aqueous solutions of CNDs, CNDs + AuNPs, and CNDs + AuNPs + TU under the daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).
Comparison of capabilities between various methods for TU detection.
Recovery experiments of spiked TU in real samples with CB set as zero.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent probe based on FRET effect between carbon nanodots and gold nanoparticles for sensitive detection of thiourea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Lihu Avenue 1800, Wuxi, 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Lihu Avenue 1800, Wuxi, 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology, Lihu Avenue 1800, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology, Lihu Avenue 1800, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Illegal abuse results in the presence of thiourea (TU) in soil, wastewater, and even fruits, which is harmful for the environment and human health. It has urgent practical significance to design an efficient and reliable probe for TU detection. Herein, a sensitive fluorescent probe with off–on response for harmful TU was reported. The probe was designed with fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect. Firstly, the CNDs were pre-combined with AuNPs and the fluorescence of CNDs was quenched due to the FRET effect. Upon addition of TU, the fluorescence of CNDs recovered due to the unbinding of CNDs and AuNPs, since the coordination interaction between TU and AuNPs is stronger than the electrostatic interaction among CNDs and AuNPs. Under the optimum parameters, a linear relationship was found between the relative fluorescence intensity of the probe and the concentration of TU in the range of 5.00 × 10-8-1.00 × 10-6 M (R2 = 0.9958), with the limit of detection (LOD) calculated to be 3.62 × 10-8 M. This proposed method is easy to operate and has excellent selectivity and sensitivity for TU, which can be effectively applied in environmental water and compound fruit-vegetable juice.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiourea (TU) is a sulfur-containing organic compound, widely used in many fields. Several optically active thioureas and their thiazole derivatives have shown promise as emerging anti-cancer agents because of their antineoplastic activities [1]. TU can also be used as a vulcanization accelerator for rubber [2], an electrodeposition additive for copper and other metals [3,4], and a spectrophotometric reagent for metals in analytical chemistry [5]. In agriculture, TU can inhibit seed dormancy and work as a fertilizer or bactericide for vegetables and fruits [6–8]. Although TU and its derivatives are of great use, illegal abuse also makes them likely to remain in soil, river water and even fruits, which brings risks to environmental safety and health of organisms. In the list of carcinogens published by the World Health Organization's International Agency for Research on Cancer, TU is classified as a Class 3 carcinogen; and TU also appears as a reasonably suspected human carcinogen on the list of carcinogens issued by the National Institute of Toxicology, part of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services. Therefore, it is significant to find a convenient and efficient method for TU monitoring.

Several methods have been proposed for TU determination, including colorimetric method [9,10], electrochemical method [11–13], chemiluminescence method [14], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [15] and fluorescence spectroscopy [16–18]. As for the electrochemical method, Rahman M. M. et al. established a linearity between the current and the concentration of TU by a glassy carbon electrode coated with the ternary co-doped Ag2O/TiO2/ZrO2 nanoparticles [13]. This electrochemical sensor not only worked over a wild operating range of 0.1 nM-0.01 mM (R2 = 0.9996) but also had a LOD as low as 91.70 ± 4.59 pM. While the preparation procedure of probes was complicated. Based on the catalysis of TU and its derivatives on the reaction between hydrogen peroxide and bromo-pyrogallol red, Dikunets M. A. et al. established a detection method for TU and its derivatives by combining the separation function of HPLC and post-column catalytic detection [15]. However, this method is less sensitive and requires expensive experimental equipment, with the LOD of 50 μM.

Among these methods, fluorometric method based on fluorescent probes has been widely used in analysis and detection for its merits of simple sample preparation, fast response and high sensitivity in recent years [19]. Generally, a fluorescent probe works in the form of fluorescence quenching (off) [20], fluorescence enhancement (on) [21], or a combination of the two (off–on) [22]. Carbon nanodots (CNDs), as an emerging fluorescent material, can be specially modified according to application requirements, which endows it with a broad application prospect [23]. In addition, the advantages of low cost, good hydrophilicity and biocompatibility are also worthy of attention. The characteristic of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) is the extremely high extinction coefficient in the visible range, which makes the absorption spectrum of AuNPs and the emission spectrum of fluorescent materials have good compatibility. Therefore, AuNPs often works as a fluorescent quencher in fluorescent systems based on FRET or the inner filter effect (IFE) [24,25].

Accordingly, this work combined the fluorescence quenching phenomenon between CNDs and AuNPs with the AuNPs-aggregation ability of TU. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that carbon nanodots pre-quenched by gold nanoparticles have been reported for TU sensing. The sensing mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Within 5.00 × 10-8-1.00 × 10-6 M, the relative fluorescence intensity of CNDs + AuNPs was demonstrated to be linearly dependent on the TU concentration under optimum detection circumstances, and the LOD was 3.62 × 10-8 M. The sensor established here owns selectivity and sensitivity above the average, which contributes to satisfactory detection ability for TU in real samples, such as tap water, lake water and compound fruit-vegetable juice.

Urea was purchased from Jinming Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Anhydrous citric acid, chloroauric acid, anhydrous trisodium citrate, silica gel (Acuity FCP. 300–400 mesh), methanol, dichloromethane, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, thiourea (TU), allantoin, melamine, dicyandiamide (DCD), biuret, guanidine hydrochloride (GH), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe), L-alanine (L-Ala), glycine (Gly), L-tyrosine (L-Tyr), DL-serine (DL-Ser), DL-aspartic acid (DL-Asp), L-valine (L-Val), L-histidine (L-His), glucose, ascorbic acid (AA), CaCl2, KCl, CuCl2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, MgCl2, ZnCl2, MnCl2·4H2O, Na2SO4, NaNO3 were all supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and the experimental water was ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm), prepared by a Labonova Smart ultrapure water system (Think-lab , Germany).

The fluorescence spectra of all samples were recorded by FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK), and the time-resolved spectra were obtained by FLS920 steady-state/transient-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured by a UV-2600 ultra-violet spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were photographed by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured by Frontier infrared spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Zeta potential was measured with a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 nanoparticle size potentiometer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The microwave oven used was M1-L202B microwave oven (Midea, China).

CNDs were synthesized by microwave method according to a previous report with some modifications [26]. Briefly, urea (1.0000 g) and citric acid (1.0000 g) were weighed into a beaker with 10.00 mL ultrapure water and agitated until fully dissolved. The above precursors were then microwaved for 3 min in a microwave oven (700 W). After cooling to ambient temperature, 25.00 mL of ultrapure water was injected to fully dissolve the product and the solution was filtered through a filter paper. Finally, a silica gel column chromatography was adopted to separate the CNDs from the filtrate and the mobile phase was a mixture of dichloromethane/methanol (8:1, V/V). After replacing the solvent of CNDs with ultrapure water, it was further diluted until the absorbance of UV–Vis absorption spectrum at 351 nm was 0.120, and the concentration of CNDs at this time was set as 1 unit. The CNDs solution was then stored at 4 °C for subsequent characterization and use.

Typically, AuNPs were obtained through the trisodium citrate reduction method with some modifications [27]. 200.00 mL of HAuCl4 solution (1.00 × 10-3 M) was heated to boiling in an oil bath, followed by a rapid injection of trisodium citrate (20.00 mL, 11.00 mg/mL) under vigorous stirring. After stirring for another 30 min at the constant temperature, the color of the reactant would change from pale yellow to wine-red. Once cooling down, the prepared AuNPs were stored at 4 °C before further characterization and use.

2.04 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 2.00 × 10-2 M, pH = 7.0), 300 μL of CNDs solution (concentration of 1 unit) and 360 μL AuNPs (1.00 × 10-8 M) were mixed well. Then, 300 μL of ultrapure water or TU solutions of different concentrations were added respectively, and the samples were shaken evenly again. After incubation for 10 min at room temperature, fluorescence spectra of these samples were recorded (λex = 405 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TU in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Real samples chosen for this section were tap water, lake water and compound fruit-vegetable juice. The tap water sample was collected from our laboratory, and the lake water sample was collected from the lake of our university. Before analysis, micropore membranes (0.22 μm) were used for filtration of both water samples. The carrot compound fruit-vegetable juice (WeiChuan, China) was obtained from a convenience store in Wuxi, China, followed by a centrifugation for 10 min at 7000 rpm to remove the contained pulp, and the upper liquid was taken as the juice sample without extra dilution. All of these real samples were analyzed using the method described in section 2.5, while for the analysis of juice sample, the concentration of AuNPs should be increased form 1.20 × 10-9 M to 2.50 × 10-9 M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of CNDs and AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of as-prepared CNDs and AuNPs were characterized by HRTEM. The nearly spherical and finely dispersed CNDs can be seen in Fig. 1 A, with a larger average lateral size of 15.5 nm compared with the average size of the product in the reference (3.0 ± 0.5 nm) [26]. Fig. 1C shows the HRTEM image of AuNPs that have an average lateral size of 13.5 nm and also appear as monodisperse spherical particles.

The information of functional groups on the surface of CNDs was illustrated by the FT-IR spectrum and XPS. Fig. 1E shows the FT-IR spectrum of CNDs, in which the absorption peaks of of C-N and N-H stretching vibrations separately appear at 1404 and 3430 cm−1. Besides, the peaks at 1190, 1718 and 3202 cm−1 are attributable to the C-O, C = O and O-H stretching vibrations, respectively. The above information indicates the existence of hydrophilic groups (–NH2 and –COOH) on the surface of the CNDs, which contributes to the good dispersibility in water [28]. As shown in Fig. 1F, there are three main peaks in the full XPS spectrum of CNDs, and the peaks at 283.82, 398.88 and 530.63 eV match with C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s respectively. According to the XPS survey, it can be known that the atomic ratio of the three elements C: N: O is about 55.54: 18.36: 26.10, which indicates that nitrogen was successfully doped into the CNDs. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. S1A) exhibits peaks at 284.82, 286.58 and 288.48 eV, corresponding to the aromatic ring C-C/C = C, C-N/C-O and C = O. Moreover, the main peak of N 1 s (Fig. S1B) can be fitted into two distinct components at 399.89 and 402.25 eV, which separately refer to the C-N and N-H bonds. In addition, peaks assigned to the C-O and C = O bonds show up at 531.63 and 533.22 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. S1C) [22,24]. The surface functional groups information of CNDs obtained from the XPS is consistent with the characterization of FT-IR, both indicating the successful doping of nitrogen and the existence of amino groups.

The Zeta potential also confirms that the surface of CNDs is rich in amino groups. As shown in Fig. 2 C, the presence of amino groups makes the CNDs positive charged(ζ = +14.8 mV), while the Zeta potential of AuNPs is negative (ζ = -30.3 mV), that is, the surface electrical properties of AuNPs are opposite to those of CNDs, presumably because the surface of AuNPs is rich in carboxyl groups [22].

Fig. 2A shows the fluorescence excitation spectra of CNDs when the wavelength of excitation was adjusted from 365 to 420 nm, and the peaks of emission spectra appear red-shifted as the increasing of excitation wavelength. When excited with 405 nm, the fluorescence emission band with the maximum relative fluorescence intensity can be obtained, and the emission peak is located at 505 nm. As for the characterization of UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2B), CNDs exhibit an absorption peak at 351 nm and the characteristic one at 520 nm is ascribed to AuNPs. The concentration of AuNPs can be quantified by the absorbance combined with Lambert-Beer's law, given that the extinction coefficient of AuNPs (particle size of 13 nm) is 2.7 × 108 (M·cm)-1 for 520 nm [29].

The proposed probe was designed with fluorescent CNDs and AuNPs. Briefly, the CNDs were pre-combined with AuNPs through electrostatic interaction and thus the fluorescence of CNDs was quenched due to the FRET effect. Upon addition of TU, the fluorescence of CNDs recovered due to the unbinding of CNDs and AuNPs, which was caused by the coordination interaction between TU and AuNPs. When AuNPs were removed from the sensing system, the relative fluorescence intensity of CNDs remained almost the same without and with TU (Fig. 3 B), which indicated that there was no interaction between CNDs and TU that would affect the fluorescence of CNDs.

The above mechanisms of the “off–on” fluorescence and corresponding interactions were further verified as follows. In view of the obvious overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of CNDs and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs in Fig. 3A, the mechanism of quenched fluorescence of CNDs could be initially narrowed down to IFE or FRET. Fig. 2D shows the fluorescence decay plots of CNDs and CNDs + AuNPs. With the addition of AuNPs, the average fluorescence lifetime of CNDs decreased from 7.93 ns to 7.44 ns, which makes the FRET effect considered to be the main mechanism of fluorescence quenching between AuNPs and CNDs in this work [30]. As for the interaction mechanism between CNDs and AuNPs, the opposite electrical properties of the two indicates that CNDs could electrostatically combined with AuNPs, which was confirmed by the increased Zeta potential of CNDs + AuNPs (ζ = -12.4 mV) in Fig. 2C.

Since the S atoms have strong binding affinity to AuNPs [31], there might be a coordination interaction between AuNPs and TU. The XPS analysis of Au 4f was applied to confirm the efficient binding of TU to AuNPs. As shown in Fig. S2, the Au 4f7/2 binding energy value of the CNDs + AuNPs corresponds to Au0 (84.14 eV), while the peak of Au 4f7/2 is shifted by + 0.34 eV with respect to the Au0 position when TU is introduced. The higher binding energy indicates that the surface Au atoms are in an oxidized state and the magnitude of the shift is consistent with the result of oxidation from Au0 to Au1+ [32,33]. In addition, since the Au 4f7/2 peak at 84.48 eV is prominent, rather than appearing as a shoulder or peak broadening, it can be inferred that most of the surface gold atoms existing as Au1+ are involved in Au-S bonding. TU triggered the aggregation of AuNPs through a coordination interaction that is stronger than the electrostatic interaction between CNDs and AuNPs, which resulted in the unbinding of CNDs and AuNPs. Therefore, the conditions for the FRET between CNDs and AuNPs were destroyed, and the quenched fluorescence appeared to be recovered by TU. As shown in Fig. 2B, CNDs + AuNPs exhibits absorption peaks of both materials at 351 nm and 524 nm. The addition of TU would not change the absorption peak of CNDs, but the characteristic absorbance at 524 nm was significantly reduced, and a new absorption peak emerged at 680 nm, which indicates the aggregation of AuNPs [29]. Meanwhile, there was a color change in the AuNPs from red to purple (or gray-blue with the increasing of TU concentration). Besides, the aggregation is intuitively reflected in the HRTEM images of the CNDs + AuNPs probe before and after adding TU, as shown in Fig. 3C and 3D.

Before establishing the characteristic sensing curve, the experimental parameters were optimized for the sake of improving the detection performance of CNDs + AuNPs, which involved the concentration of AuNPs, pH value and incubation time.

With fixed concentrations of CNDs (1 unit) and TU (1.00 × 10-7 M), the level of fluorescence recovery was investigated when the AuNPs concentration was in the range of 5.00 × 10-10-3.00 × 10-9 M. Respectively, (F0-F)/F0 and (F'-F)/F are defined as the fluorescence quenching and enhanced efficiency, where F0 represents the relative fluorescence intensity of CNDs, with F and F' corresponding to the relative fluorescence intensity of CNDs + AuNPs and CNDs + AuNPs + TU. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of CNDs increased with the increase of AuNPs concentration, and it tended to be stabilized when the AuNPs concentration was more than 2.00 × 10-9 M (Fig. S3A). Besides, the highest fluorescence enhancement efficiency was obtained when the AuNPs concentration was 1.20 × 10-9 M (Fig. S3B). Therefore, the concentration of AuNPs was immobilized at 1.20 × 10-9 M in the follow-up experiments. In addition, Fig. S3C exhibits the performance of three CNDs + AuNPs probes with different AuNPs concentrations (5.00 × 10-10 M, 1.20 × 10-9 M, 3.00 × 10-9 M). This also indicates that a low AuNPs concentration (5.00 × 10-10 M) would inhibit the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CNDs, which might compress the linear range of sensing results. However, when the AuNPs concentration is too high (3.00 × 10-9 M), the detection ability for TU of low concentration is inhibited, which is not conducive to lowering the limit of detection.

The effect of pH value on the sensing process was explored experimentally. The samples of pH 6.0 and 7.0 were prepared with PBS (2.00 × 10-2 M), and samples of the remaining pH values were prepared with HCl and NaOH directly. The fluorescence intensity of CNDs fluctuated slightly in the pH range of 3.0 ∼ 8.0, and then dropped sharply with the further increasing of pH value (Fig. S4A). Fig. S4B and S4C show that the fluorescence quenching efficiency increased first and then decreased when the solution became more alkaline, with the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency obtained at pH 8.0. Besides, the fluorescence enhancement efficiency also showed the tendency to increase and then decrease in the pH range of 4.0 ∼ 12.0, with a maximum fluorescence enhancement efficiency at pH = 7.0. Since the fluorescence intensity of CNDs and the fluorescence quenching efficiency were almost the same at pH 7.0 and 8.0, and the fluorescence recovery efficiency directly reflected the detection ability of the fluorescent probe, so that the pH value was fixed at 7.0 for the optimal performance of CNDs + AuNPs.

Fig. S5A shows the relative fluorescence intensity at 505 nm of the CNDs + AuNPs probe in the absence and presence of 1.00 × 10-7 M TU within 20 min. It can be seen that the two interaction processes were basically stable after 2 min. When the concentration of TU was increased to 1.00 × 10-6 M (Fig. S5B), the reactant took a longer time, about 5 min, to reach equilibrium and remained stable for at least 55 min. The rapid aggregation is consistent with the phenomenon in previous studies [19,34]. In order to ensure the reliability of results, the incubation time of the proposed method was selected as 10 min.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the CNDs + AuNPs probe with TU of different concentrations (Fig. 4 A) were collected under the optimized detection conditions. Fig. 4B describes the relevance of the relative fluorescence intensity (505 nm) of CNDs + AuNPs to the concentration of TU within 1.00 × 10-5 M. With the increasing concentration of TU, the relative fluorescence intensity of the probe increased first and then tended to be stabilized when the TU concentration exceeded 3.00 × 10-6 M. The relative fluorescence intensity showed a linear correlation with the concentration of TU in the range of 5.00 × 10-8-1.00 × 10-6 M, and the calibration curve can be expressed as y = 27270.8593x + 30253.6833 (R2 = 0.9958), where y represents the relative intensity of fluorescence emission at 505 nm and x represents the concentration of TU. The LOD was calculated to be 3.62 × 10-8 M according to the following formula
LOD = 3
s b
S
where sb is the standard deviation of the blank measures and S (sensitivity) is the slope of calibration curve.

Detection capabilities for TU of this work and other reported methods were compared in Table 1 , and the conditions corresponding to the results of each method can be found in Table S1. Except for the electrochemical method using Ag2O/TiO2/ZrO2/GCE and the colorimetric method using the complex of (2-pridylazo) resorcinol (PAR) and Hg2+, the LOD of this sensor is in the same order of magnitude as that of most methods, and this method is more convenient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of CNDs + AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of CNDs + AuNPs, nitrogen-rich analogues (allantoin, urea, melamine, dicyandiamide, biuret, GH), common amino acids (L-Phe, L-Ala, Gly, L-Tyr, DL-Ser, DL-Asp, L-Val, L-His) and other coexisting substances (glucose, AA, Ca2+, K+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, SO4 2-, NO3 –) were selected as interferents. In this section, the concentrations of TU and other nitrogen-rich analogues were fixed at 2.00 × 10-6 M, and the concentrations of common amino acids, glucose, AA and ions were set at 1.00 × 10-5 M during the exploration. As shown in Fig. 5 A, melamine could also make the CNDs + AuNPs probe show a significant recovery of fluorescence, while the fluorescence recovery efficiency of melamine was much lower than that of TU. As can be seen from Fig. 5B, except Cu2+ and Fe3+, when there were coexisting substances such as nitrogen-rich analogues with the same concentration as TU, amino acids or ions with the concentration of 5 times that of TU, the TU detection ability of the CNDs + AuNPs probe was basically not affected. Both Cu2+ and Fe3+ have a certain inhibitory effect on the fluorescence recovery ability of TU, thus masking agents should be introduced to eliminate their adverse effects in practical applications. The excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability presented by the above results promise the CNDs + AuNPs probe great potential for TU detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TU in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of CNDs + AuNPs fluorescent probes for TU detection in real samples was evaluated, with tap water, lake water and compound fruit-vegetable juice selected as practical samples. The equation established in Section 3.5 is not suitable for the determination of TU in these real samples because of the existence of different interferents. The results of standard addition experiments (Fig. S6) show that linear relationships can be established between the fluorescence signal and the spiked concentration of TU in these samples, which indicates the potential application of this method in these samples. And the different linear relationships in different samples are confirmed by the different slopes of the linear fitting equations established in each sample.

In the following discussion, CA, CB and CC refers to the actual concentration, the inherent background concentration of sample and the concentration converted from fluorescence signal respectively. In a certain sample, the existence of CB directly enlarged the corresponding concentrations of each fluorescence signal and therefore decreased the intercepts of linear fitting equations, but has no influence on the slopes and limits of detection, which are calculated without CB. The limits of detection were 2.50 × 10-8 M, 1.70 × 10-8 M and 5.60 × 10-8 M in tap water, lake water and compound fruit-vegetable juice respectively. Within the linear range, CC is close to the spiked concentration and there exists the following relationship
C A
≈
C B
+
C C

which indicates that the CA can be obtained only if the CB is known. In other words, before applying this method for the detection of TU in a real sample, it is necessary to establish the relationship between the fluorescence signal and the concentration of TU in the corresponding sample where the CB of TU is known.

Setting CB as zero will not negatively affect the results of recovery experiments. On the contrary, if CB is included in the calculation of recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD), the recovery will be closer to 100% and the RSD will be smaller (See Supplementary Data for details). When the CB is set as zero, the results of recovery experiments with spiked TU (3.00 × 10-7 M, 5.00 × 10-7 M, 7.00 × 10-7 M) in these samples are summarized in Table 2 . The acceptable recoveries between 95.8 and 103% were obtained, and the RSD was in the range of 0.63–8.02%. These results indicate that the CNDs + AuNPs fluorescent probe owns a broad prospect in monitoring TU content in real samples.

In summary, this work established a CNDs + AuNPs fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of harmful TU. The fluorescence signal generated from CNDs was quenched by AuNPs on account of the FRET effect. Once introduced, TU would trigger the aggregation of AuNPs through Au-S bond and thus destroy the essential conditions of the FRET effect, then the quenched fluorescence signal would show a recovery in the presence of TU. In the best performance, a linear range of 5.00 × 10-8-1.00 × 10-6 M and the LOD of 3.62 × 10-8 M was obtained. The method established in this work has the advantages of convenience, good selectivity and high sensitivity. Besides, its application prospects are also illustrated by the results of detecting TU in real samples, such as tap water, lake water and compound fruit-vegetable juice.

Anqi Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Guoqing Chen: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Taiqun Yang: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Chaoqun Ma: Resources. Lei Li: Supervision. Hui Gao: Data curation. Jiao Gu: Resources. Chun Zhu: Investigation. Yamin Wu: Data curation. Xiaolin Li: Writing – review & editing. Yitao Wei: Writing – review & editing. Anlan Huang: Visualization. Xiaoqian Qiu: Visualization. Jinzeng Xu: Visualization. Jialu Shen: Visualization. Lvyuan Zhong: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China [2018YFC1604204-3] and the Key Research and Development Program of Jiangsu Province [No. BE2020756].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121582.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
